title: Wave Accounting Documentation
prompt_source: Inline docs prompt; CONNECTOR_DOCUMENTATION_GUIDELINES fetched via getDocGuidelines()

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Wave** is an accounting + invoicing SaaS exposing a single **GraphQL endpoint** rather than a REST surface. This connector â€” `WaveConnector` (`CONNECTOR_TYPE = "wave"`, `AUTH_TYPE = "api_key"`) â€” wraps the operational surfaces a Shielva tenant typically needs from a Wave business:

| Surface | GraphQL ops | Capability |
|---|---|---|
| Identity | `user` | Authenticated user node |
| Businesses | `businesses`, `business(id)` | List + read businesses |
| Customers | `business.customers`, `customerCreate` | List + create customers |
| Invoices | `business.invoices`, `invoiceCreate` | List + create invoices |
| Products | `business.products`, `productCreate` | List + create products |
| Accounts | `business.accounts` | Read chart of accounts |
| Transactions | `business.transactions` | List ledger transactions |
| Sales Taxes | `business.salesTaxes` | List sales-tax rates |

### Why GraphQL not REST

Wave's only public surface is `POST https://gql.waveapps.com/graphql/public` with a body of `{"query": "...", "variables": {...}}`. Unlike REST, the *operation name* lives in the request body, not the URL. The connector keeps every query string in `helpers/queries.py` (SOC â€” `connector.py` never embeds GraphQL literals) and routes each through `client/http_client.py::WaveHTTPClient.execute(query, variables, context)`.

The connector normalises **customers + invoices** into `NormalizedDocument` (id = `f"{tenant_id}_{source_id}"`), surfaces standalone `async def` methods per user-requested operation (OCP), retries 429/5xx with exponential backoff (3 attempts), inspects GraphQL `errors[]` even on HTTP 200, and never embeds raw HTTP or query strings in `connector.py`.

sections.title: Quick Start
sections.content:
### 1. Generate a Wave personal access token

1. Sign in to https://my.waveapps.com.
2. Open **Settings â†’ API Access â†’ Manage Applications**.
3. Click **Create token** (or open an existing app and generate a full-access token).
4. Copy the token value â€” Wave shows it only once.
5. Optional: from **Settings â†’ Business Profile**, copy the **Business ID** if you want to scope this connector to a single business by default.

### 2. Install in ACP

In **ACP â†’ Manage Connectors â†’ Wave Accounting**, fill in:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `access_token` (secret) | Personal access token from step 4 |
| `business_id` (string, optional) | Default Business ID â€” methods accept a per-call override |
| `base_url` (string, optional) | Override `https://gql.waveapps.com/graphql/public` (rarely needed) |
| `rate_limit_per_min` (number) | Soft cap, default 60 |

Click **Install**. `install()` validates `access_token` is present (it does NOT call the API), initialises the `WaveHTTPClient` with a `Bearer` header, and returns `ConnectorStatus(HEALTHY, AUTHENTICATED)`.

### 3. First health check

The gateway calls `health_check()`, which posts a minimal `{ user { id } }` GraphQL query. On 2xx â†’ `HEALTHY + CONNECTED`. On 401 â†’ `OFFLINE + TOKEN_EXPIRED`. On 403 â†’ `UNHEALTHY + FAILED`. On 429 â†’ `DEGRADED + CONNECTED` with a structured-log warning.

sections.title: Authentication
sections.content:
Wave's public API uses **personal-access-token authentication** (`AUTH_TYPE = "api_key"`). No OAuth dance, no token refresh, no expiry.

### Credential model

- `access_token` is sent in the `Authorization` header as `Bearer <token>` â€” this is the standard form, *not* the raw-header form used by some providers (Wix sends the key raw with no `Bearer ` prefix; Wave does the opposite).
- `business_id` is sent as a GraphQL **variable** in the query body, not a header. It's optional at install time; methods that need it accept a per-call override.

### Lifecycle

| Phase | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `install()` | Validates `access_token`. Initialises `WaveHTTPClient`. Does **not** call any Wave endpoint. |
| `authorize(...)` | Returns a `TokenInfo(access_token=<the same token>, token_type="api_key")` â€” there is no token exchange. |
| `health_check()` | `POST { "query": "{ user { id } }", "variables": {} }` â€” minimal probe. |
| `ensure_token()` | Not called â€” no token to refresh. |
| token storage | None on the platform side; the token lives only in `self.config["access_token"]`. |

### Header contract

Every outbound request:

```
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
Content-Type:  application/json
Accept:        application/json
```

### GraphQL request envelope

Every operation â€” query or mutation â€” POSTs:

```json
{
  "query":     "<one of the strings in helpers/queries.py>",
  "variables": { ... }
}
```

Successful response:

```json
{ "data": { "<op>": { ... } } }
```

Partial / total failure on HTTP 200 (GraphQL spec):

```json
{ "data": null, "errors": [ { "message": "...", "extensions": { "code": "..." } } ] }
```

The client inspects `errors[]` on every payload â€” even HTTP 200 â€” and raises `WaveError` so the orchestrator can rely on `data` being well-formed.

sections.title: Configuration
sections.content:
### Install fields (per-tenant)

Read via `self.config.get(...)` in `connector.py::__init__` â€” never hardcoded.

| Key | Type | Required | Read in code | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `access_token` | secret | yes | `self.access_token` | Bearer token sent in Authorization header |
| `business_id` | string | no | `self.default_business_id` | Default Business ID; per-call arg can override |
| `base_url` | string | no | `self.base_url` (defaults to `https://gql.waveapps.com/graphql/public`) | Override for Wave sandbox or proxy |
| `rate_limit_per_min` | number | no | `self.rate_limit_per_min` (default 60) | Client-side soft cap |

### Class constants (provider-wide)

```python
REQUIRED_CONFIG_KEYS = ["access_token"]
_STATUS_MAP = {
    401: ("OFFLINE",   "TOKEN_EXPIRED"),
    403: ("UNHEALTHY", "INVALID_CREDENTIALS"),
    429: ("DEGRADED",  "CONNECTED"),
}
_WAVE_BASE = "https://gql.waveapps.com/graphql/public"
```

sections.title: API Methods
sections.content: All public async methods on `WaveConnector`. SOC: connector.py orchestrates only; HTTP lives in `client/http_client.py::WaveHTTPClient`; query strings live in `helpers/queries.py`.

children.title: Lifecycle
children.content:
### `async install() -> ConnectorStatus`
Validate `access_token`; initialise `WaveHTTPClient`. Does NOT call the API.

### `async authorize(auth_code, state) -> TokenInfo`
No-op for api_key auth â€” returns a TokenInfo whose access_token is the configured personal access token.

### `async health_check() -> ConnectorStatus`
Probes `{ user { id } }`. Classifies failures by `_STATUS_MAP`.

### `async sync(since=None, full=False, kb_id=None, webhook_url=None) -> SyncResult`
Iterates Wave customers + invoices across every accessible business â†’ `NormalizedDocument` â†’ `ingest_document`. Returns `SyncResult` with `documents_synced / _failed / _found`.

children.title: Identity
children.content:
### `async get_user() -> Dict`
GraphQL `{ user { id defaultEmail firstName lastName } }` â€” returns the `user` sub-object.

### `async list_users() -> Dict`
Wave's public schema exposes only the authenticated user â€” this method wraps `get_user` in a connection-style envelope (`{ users: { pageInfo, edges: [{ node: user }] } }`) so list-page callers can treat it uniformly with the other `list_*` methods.

children.title: Businesses
children.content:
### `async list_businesses(*, page=1, page_size=50) -> Dict`
GraphQL `businesses(page: $page, pageSize: $pageSize)`.

### `async get_business(business_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id: $id)`. Uses the configured `default_business_id` when no arg is passed.

children.title: Customers
children.content:
### `async list_customers(business_id=None, *, page=1, page_size=50) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).customers(page, pageSize)`.

### `async get_customer(customer_id, business_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).customer(id)`.

### `async create_customer(name, email=None, business_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `customerCreate(input: CustomerCreateInput!)` mutation. The connector wraps the simple `name` + `email` args into Wave's `CustomerCreateInput` shape: `{ businessId, name, email? }`.

children.title: Invoices
children.content:
### `async list_invoices(business_id=None, *, page=1, page_size=50, status=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).invoices(page, pageSize, status)`. `status` is uppercased before sending (e.g. `"paid"` â†’ `"PAID"`).

### `async get_invoice(invoice_id, business_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).invoice(id)` â€” also fetches `items[]` for the requested invoice.

### `async create_invoice(customer_id, items, business_id=None, due_date=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `invoiceCreate(input: InvoiceCreateInput!)` mutation. `items` is a list of `{product_id, quantity?, unit_price?, description?}` dicts; the connector translates snake_case â†’ camelCase (Wave's `InvoiceCreateItemInput` uses `productId`, `unitPrice`).

children.title: Products
children.content:
### `async list_products(business_id=None, *, page=1, page_size=50) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).products(page, pageSize)`.

### `async create_product(name, unit_price, business_id=None, *, description=None, income_account_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `productCreate(input: ProductCreateInput!)` mutation.

children.title: Accounts Â· Transactions Â· Sales taxes
children.content:
### `async list_accounts(business_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).accounts` â€” full chart of accounts.

### `async list_transactions(business_id=None, *, from_date=None, to_date=None, page=1, page_size=50) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).transactions(page, pageSize, from, to)`. Date args are ISO `YYYY-MM-DD`.

### `async list_sales_taxes(business_id=None) -> Dict`
GraphQL `business(id).salesTaxes` â€” sales-tax rate registry.

sections.title: Error Handling
sections.content:
Exception hierarchy in `exceptions.py`:

```
WaveError                              # base; carries status_code + response_body
â”śâ”€â”€ WaveAuthError                      # 401 / 403
â”śâ”€â”€ WaveBadRequestError                # 400
â”śâ”€â”€ WaveValidationError                # client-side input validation
â”śâ”€â”€ WaveNotFoundError                  # 404
â”śâ”€â”€ WaveRateLimitError                 # 429 â€” retry_after_s
â””â”€â”€ WaveServerError                    # 5xx
```

Back-compat aliases preserved:
```
WaveNetworkError = WaveServerError     # legacy name from older code
WaveNotFound     = WaveNotFoundError
```

### GraphQL-level errors (HTTP 200 + errors[])

The GraphQL spec allows a 200 OK response to still carry partial validation / authorization errors in `errors[]`. `WaveHTTPClient._post` inspects this on every payload and raises `WaveError` carrying the joined `errors[*].message` strings so `connector.py` never has to look at `errors[]` itself.

### Retry behaviour (`client/http_client.py::_post`)

| Status | Action |
|---|---|
| 400 / 401 / 403 / 404 | Raise immediately |
| 429 | Exponential backoff `_BACKOFF_BASE * 2 ** attempt` (0.5s, 1s, 2s), retry up to `_MAX_RETRIES=3` |
| 5xx | Same exponential backoff, retry up to 3 |
| `httpx.TimeoutException` / `httpx.NetworkError` | Backoff, retry up to 3, then raise `WaveNetworkError` |

### How errors surface to the gateway

`health_check()` catches exceptions and maps via `_STATUS_MAP`:

```
401 â†’ ConnectorStatus(OFFLINE,   TOKEN_EXPIRED)
403 â†’ ConnectorStatus(UNHEALTHY, FAILED)
429 â†’ ConnectorStatus(DEGRADED,  CONNECTED)   â€” logged as warning
5xx â†’ ConnectorStatus(OFFLINE,   CONNECTED)
other â†’ ConnectorStatus(DEGRADED, CONNECTED)
```

sections.title: Troubleshooting
sections.content:
| Symptom | Probable cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `ConnectorStatus(OFFLINE, MISSING_CREDENTIALS)` at install | `access_token` blank | Re-enter the token in ACP. Wave shows the value only once at generation â€” rotate if lost. |
| `ConnectorStatus(UNHEALTHY, FAILED)` at health_check | 403 from Wave â€” token lacks scope or was revoked | In Wave Dashboard â†’ API Access, generate a new full-access token with the required scopes. |
| `ConnectorStatus(OFFLINE, TOKEN_EXPIRED)` at health_check | 401 â€” token revoked or wrong | Regenerate the token in Wave Dashboard and re-enter it in ACP. |
| `wave.http.retry` warnings spike | Hitting 429 (Wave's per-minute rate cap) | The client retries with exponential backoff. Lower `rate_limit_per_min` or batch fewer requests. |
| `WaveError: GraphQL errors: business not found` on every call | Wrong `business_id` | Call `list_businesses()` to confirm the available IDs, then update the install field. |
| `WaveValidationError: business_id is required` | No `business_id` arg and no `default_business_id` configured | Pass `business_id=` on every call OR set the default in ACP. |
| `WaveError: GraphQL errors: Cannot query field ...` after Wave schema change | Schema drift between this connector and live Wave API | Update `helpers/queries.py` â€” every operation string lives there. |
| `ImportError: cannot import name 'WaveNetworkError'` | Older `exceptions.py` without back-compat aliases | Already fixed â€” `WaveNetworkError = WaveServerError` alias added in `exceptions.py`. |
